question.text: According to the landmark Brinson, Hood & Beebower (1986) study, which of the following statements about the determinants of portfolio return variation is CORRECT?

answer.text: Security selection explains 90% of the variation in a fund's returns over time
Asset allocation explains 90% of the variation in returns across different portfolios
Asset allocation explains about 90% of the variation in a single fund's returns over time, but only about 40% of the variation across different portfolios
Market timing explains 90% of returns while asset allocation explains only 10%

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The BHB study found that asset allocation policy explains approximately 90% of the variability of a fund's returns over time (time-series variation). However, when looking across all portfolios, asset allocation explains only about 40% of the variation in returns (cross-sectional variation). These are two different statistical questions. **The Trap:** The most common exam error is confusing "variation over time" (90%) with "variation across portfolios" (40%). Option B cleverly reverses the context. Option A attributes the 90% to security selection instead of asset allocation. **Cross-Exam Context:** The BHB study is referenced in NISM Series X-A, X-B, and is also a key concept in NISM Series V-A (Mutual Fund Distributors) exam. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This study is the foundation of why advisers spend most time on getting asset allocation right rather than picking individual securities — the big picture decision matters far more than stock-picking for any single portfolio.
